description_long: This sculpture features a pair of stylized guardian lion-dog figures, known as Shisa or Komainu, crafted from a dark, weathered stone or ceramic material. Each figure is seated upright with muscular bodies, prominent swirling patterns on their shoulders, haunches, and tails, and expressive, fierce facial features including wide eyes, flared nostrils, and open mouths. The tails are curled and pointed, and the figures are positioned side by side on a rectangular stone base. The surface is textured with intricate carved details, especially in the mane and tail, giving a sense of movement and energy. The coloration is predominantly a deep brown with lighter highlights accentuating the sculpted swirls and contours. This type of sculpture is commonly found at entrances to temples, shrines, or public spaces in East Asia, serving as symbolic protectors.
description: A pair of intricately carved guardian lion-dog figures with swirling patterns, seated side by side on a stone base, made from dark, weathered material.
description_view_0: Front view showing both lion-dog figures' expressive faces, open mouths, and detailed swirling patterns on their chests and shoulders.
description_view_1: Side view highlighting the curled tails, muscular haunches, and the depth of the swirling patterns along the bodies.
description_view_2: Rear view displaying the backs and tails of both figures, with the swirling patterns most prominent on the tails and hindquarters.
description_view_3: Opposite side view focusing on the tails and the side profiles of the heads, with less facial detail visible.
attribute_subject_matter: pair of guardian lion-dog figures (Shisa or Komainu)
attribute_location_context: The sculpture is placed on a flat stone base, suggesting an outdoor or semi-outdoor setting such as a plaza, temple entrance, or park.
